chunk_summary: This section addresses the management of borderline anthropometric values and discordant metrics in outpatient care, emphasizing technical validation, clinical risk stratification, and follow-up strategies for malnourished children.
learning_objectives: Understand the significance of borderline MUAC and WHZ values in malnutrition risk assessment.
Learn technical steps to confirm anthropometric measurements and address discordance.
Identify clinical risk factors for prioritizing intensive dietary support and follow-up.
Explore strategies for managing children with borderline metrics in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Borderline MUAC values: 124–125 mm; WHZ near -2 are common indicators of malnutrition risk.
Mortality risk is highest when both MUAC and WHZ are low, requiring intensified follow-up.
WHO recommends prioritizing children with MUAC 115–119 mm, WAZ < -3, age < 24 months, or comorbidities like HIV or TB.
Technical validation includes repeating measurements, confirming equipment calibration, and ensuring proper technique.
In clinics without specialized foods, tighter follow-up and linkage to supplementary feeding are essential for high-risk children.
reference_quote: WHO provides practical risk factors to decide when counselling alone is unlikely to be sufficient and when children should be prioritized for more intensive dietary support and closer follow-up: MUAC 115–119 mm, weight-for-age (WAZ) < -3, age <24 months, failure to recover after other interventions, relapse or prior severe wasting, comorbidity (for example HIV, TB, disability), and severe adverse social circumstances.
